nodes.text: The biggest lesson from 70 years of AI research: general methods that leverage computation win, by a large margin. Researchers have always tried to build in human knowledge—it helps short-term, is personally satisfying, but plateaus and inhibits progress. We are still making the same mistakes. The bitter lesson: search and learning win. Build meta-methods that discover complexity, not encode it.

nodes.text: The biggest lesson from 70 years of AI research: general methods leveraging computation are ultimately the most effective, by a large margin. Moore's law means exponentially falling cost per unit of computation. Researchers seek short-term gains by leveraging human knowledge, but only the leveraging of computation matters in the long run.

nodes.text: In chess, the methods that defeated Kasparov in 1997 were based on massive, deep search. The human-knowledge researchers were not good losers—they said "brute force" search was not a general strategy, not how people played. The same pattern repeated in Go, speech recognition, and vision. Search and learning are the two most important classes of techniques for utilizing massive computation.

nodes.text: The two methods that scale arbitrarily are search and learning. The contents of minds are tremendously, irredeemably complex—we should stop trying to find simple ways to think about them. Build only meta-methods that find and capture this complexity. We want AI agents that can discover like we can, not which contain what we have discovered.

nodes.text: The biggest lesson from 70 years of AI research: general methods that leverage computation are the most effective, by a large margin. The reason is Moore's law — exponential fall in cost per unit of computation. Most AI research proceeds as if computation were fixed, making human knowledge the only lever. But massively more computation becomes available over time. Researchers pursue short-term gains through domain knowledge; in the long run, computation is all that matters. Human-knowledge methods complicate approaches in ways that resist scaling.

nodes.text: In computer chess, the methods that defeated Kasparov in 1997 were based on massive, deep search. Most chess researchers had pursued human-understanding methods and were not good losers when a search-based approach proved vastly more effective. They called it brute force, said it wasn't general, said it wasn't how people played chess. They wanted their approach to win.

nodes.text: Computer Go repeated the pattern 20 years later. Enormous effort went into exploiting human knowledge and the game's special features; all proved irrelevant once search was applied at scale. Learning by self-play proved equally important — it, too, enables massive computation to be brought to bear. Search and learning are the two most important techniques in AI, and in both games, researchers found far greater success only by embracing them.

The statistical methods won. Deep learning is the latest step: even less human knowledge, even more computation, dramatically better results. Researchers always tried to build systems that worked the way their own minds worked. It proved counterproductive — a colossal waste of time.

The success is tinged with bitterness — it is success over a favored, human-centric approach.

nodes.text: Search and learning are the methods that scale arbitrarily. The actual contents of minds are irredeemably complex — stop trying to encode them. Build meta-methods that find and capture that complexity instead. We want AI agents that can discover like we can, not agents containing what we have already discovered. Building in our discoveries makes the discovering process harder to see.

nodes.text: The biggest lesson from 70 years of AI research is that general methods leveraging computation are ultimately the most effective, and by a large margin. The reason is Moore's law — the continued exponentially falling cost per unit of computation. Most AI research has been conducted as if computation were constant, making human knowledge one of the only levers for improvement. But over a slightly longer horizon than a typical research project, massively more computation becomes available. Researchers, seeking short-term gains, leverage domain knowledge — but the only thing that matters in the long run is computation. These two approaches need not conflict, but in practice they do: time on one is time not on the other, and human-knowledge methods tend to complicate things in ways that make them less suited to general computational scaling. There have been many examples of researchers learning this lesson late, and bitterly.

nodes.text: In computer chess, the methods that defeated Kasparov in 1997 were based on massive, deep search. Most chess researchers had pursued methods leveraging human understanding of chess's special structure. When a simpler search-based approach proved vastly more effective, they were not good losers. They said brute-force search may have won, but it was not a general strategy, and it was not how people played chess. They wanted human-knowledge methods to win and were disappointed when they did not.

nodes.text: A similar pattern emerged in computer Go, delayed by 20 years. Enormous efforts went into avoiding search by exploiting human knowledge and the special features of the game — and all of it proved irrelevant, or worse, once search was applied at scale. Learning by self-play was also crucial: it enabled massive computation to be brought to bear on learning a value function, as it was in many other games. Search and learning are the two most important classes of techniques for utilizing massive computation. In Go, as in chess, researchers first directed effort toward human understanding, and only much later found greater success by embracing search and learning.

nodes.text: In speech recognition, a DARPA competition in the 1970s pitted knowledge-based methods against newer statistical methods — hidden Markov models — that did more computation. The statistical methods won. This triggered a major change across natural language processing, where statistics and computation came to dominate the field over decades. The rise of deep learning in speech is the most recent step in the same direction: even less human knowledge, even more computation, learning on huge training sets, dramatically better results. Researchers always tried to build systems that worked the way they thought their own minds worked — and it proved counterproductive, a colossal waste of time, when Moore's law made massive computation available and a means was found to use it.

nodes.text: This is a big lesson. As a field, we still have not thoroughly learned it — we continue to make the same mistakes. To resist them, we have to understand their appeal. We have to learn the bitter lesson that building in how we think we think does not work in the long run. The lesson rests on four historical observations: AI researchers have often tried to build knowledge into their agents; this always helps in the short term and is personally satisfying; but in the long run it plateaus and even inhibits progress; and breakthrough progress eventually arrives by an opposing approach based on scaling computation through search and learning. That success is tinged with bitterness because it is success over a favored, human-centric approach.

nodes.text: One thing to learn from this: the great power of general-purpose methods that continue to scale with increased computation even as computation becomes very great. The two methods that seem to scale arbitrarily are search and learning.

nodes.text: The second point: the actual contents of minds are tremendously, irredeemably complex. We should stop trying to find simple ways to think about space, objects, multiple agents, symmetries. These are part of the arbitrary, intrinsically complex outside world. They are not what should be built in; instead we should build only the meta-methods that can find and capture this complexity. The search for good approximations should be by our methods, not by us. We want AI agents that can discover like we can, not which contain what we have discovered. Building in our discoveries only makes it harder to see how the discovering process can be done.

nodes.text: The Bitter Lesson
The Bitter Lesson
Rich Sutton
March 13, 2019

The biggest lesson that can be read from 70 years of AI research is
that general methods that leverage computation are ultimately the most
effective, and by a large margin. The ultimate reason for this is
Moore's law, or rather its generalization of continued exponentially
falling cost per unit of computation. Most AI research has been
conducted as if the computation available to the agent were constant
(in which case leveraging human knowledge would be one of the only ways
to improve performance) but, over a slightly longer time than a typical
research project, massively more computation inevitably becomes
available. Seeking an improvement that makes a difference in the
shorter term, researchers seek to leverage their human knowledge of the
domain, but the only thing that matters in the long run is the
leveraging of computation. These two need not run counter to each
other, but in practice they tend to. Time spent on one is time not
spent on the other. There are psychological commitments to investment
in one approach or the other. And the human-knowledge approach tends to
complicate methods in ways that make them less suited to taking
advantage of general methods leveraging computation.  There were
many examples of AI researchers' belated learning of this bitter
lesson,
and it is instructive to review some of the most prominent.

nodes.text:
In computer chess, the methods that defeated the world champion,
Kasparov, in 1997, were based on massive, deep search. At the time,
this was looked upon with dismay by the majority of computer-chess
researchers who had pursued methods that leveraged human understanding
of the special structure of chess. When a simpler, search-based
approach with special hardware and software proved vastly more
effective, these human-knowledge-based chess researchers were not good
losers. They said that ``brute force" search may have won this time,
but it was not a general strategy, and anyway it was not how people
played chess. These researchers wanted methods based on human input to
win and were disappointed when they did not.

win and were disappointed when they

nodes.text:
A similar pattern of research progress was seen in computer Go, only
delayed by a further 20 years. Enormous initial efforts went into
avoiding search by taking advantage of human knowledge, or of the
special features of the game, but all those efforts proved irrelevant,
or worse, once search was applied effectively at scale. Also important
was the use of learning by self play to learn a value function (as it
was in many other games and even in chess, although learning did not
play a big role in the 1997 program that first beat a world champion).
Learning by self play, and learning in general, is like search in that
it enables massive computation to be brought to bear. Search and
learning are the two most important classes of techniques for utilizing
massive amounts of computation in AI research. In computer Go, as in
computer chess, researchers' initial effort was directed towards
utilizing human understanding (so that less search was needed) and only
much later was much greater success had by embracing search and
learning.

nodes.text:
In speech recognition, there was an early competition, sponsored by
DARPA, in the 1970s. Entrants included a host of special methods that
took
advantage of human knowledge---knowledge of words, of phonemes, of the
human vocal tract, etc. On the other side were newer methods that were
more statistical in nature and did much more computation, based on
hidden Markov models (HMMs). Again, the statistical methods won out
over the human-knowledge-based methods. This led to a major change in
all of natural language processing, gradually over decades, where
statistics and computation came to dominate the field. The recent rise
of deep learning in speech recognition is the most recent step in this
consistent direction. Deep learning methods rely even less on human
knowledge, and use even more computation, together with learning on
huge training sets, to produce dramatically better speech recognition
systems. As in the games, researchers always tried to make systems that
worked the way the researchers thought their own minds worked---they
tried to put that knowledge in their systems---but it proved ultimately
counterproductive, and a colossal waste of researcher's time, when,
through Moore's law, massive computation became available and a means
was found to put it to good use.

nodes.text:
In computer vision, there has been a similar pattern. Early methods
conceived of vision as searching for edges, or generalized cylinders,
or in terms of SIFT features. But today all this is discarded. Modern
deep-learning neural networks use only the notions of convolution and
certain kinds of invariances, and perform much better.

use only the notions of convolution

nodes.text:
This is a big lesson. As a field, we still have not thoroughly learned
it, as we are continuing to make the same kind of mistakes. To see
this, and to effectively resist it, we have to understand the appeal of
these mistakes. We have to learn the bitter lesson that building in how
we think we think does not work in the long run. The bitter lesson is
based on the historical observations that 1) AI researchers have often
tried to build knowledge into their agents, 2) this always helps in the
short term, and is personally satisfying to the researcher, but 3) in
the long run it plateaus and even inhibits further progress, and 4)
breakthrough progress eventually arrives by an opposing approach based
on scaling computation by search and learning. The eventual success is
tinged with bitterness, and often incompletely digested, because it is
success over a favored, human-centric approach.

nodes.text:
One thing that should be learned from the bitter lesson is the great
power of general purpose methods, of methods that continue to scale
with increased computation even as the available computation becomes
very great. The two methods that seem to scale arbitrarily in this way
are search and learning.

nodes.text:
The second general point to be learned from the bitter lesson is that
the actual contents of minds are tremendously, irredeemably complex; we
should stop trying to find simple ways to think about the contents of
minds, such as simple ways to think about space, objects, multiple
agents, or symmetries. All these are part of the arbitrary,
intrinsically-complex, outside world. They are not what should be built
in, as their complexity is endless; instead we should build in only the
meta-methods that can find and capture this arbitrary complexity.
Essential to these methods is that they can find good approximations,
but the search for them should be by our methods, not by us. We want AI
agents that can discover like we can, not which contain what we have
discovered. Building in our discoveries only makes it harder to see how
the discovering process can be done.
